outline.key_point: Had a laparoscopy that 'found nothing' but you're still in pain?
It's a devastatingly common experience. A 'clear' diagnostic lap isn't always the final word, because endometriosis can be missed if a surgeon doesn't know where and how to look.
One way to prepare is to ask your surgeon for a '360-degree assessment'. This is a commitment to a thorough inspection, not just a quick look.
This means checking everywhere, including the diaphragm, appendix, bladder, and all of the bowel. You can also ask for pictures to confirm every area was checked.
Endo can hide in folds of tissue, like the Pouch of Douglas behind the uterus. A specialist will physically move organs and lift tissue to find it.
The other crucial question is *how* any endo found will be removed. Ask your surgeon if they perform excision, not just ablation.
Ablation ('burning') only treats the surface and can leave deeper disease behind. Excision ('cutting out') aims to remove the endometriosis completely.

outline.key_point: Waiting for a gynaecologist referral? You might be able to access an endometriosis nurse directly.
Access to specialist endometriosis nurses in the UK isn't uniform. Pathways can be confusing and depend on where you live.
In some parts of the UK, you don't have to wait for a specialist. In Cardiff, you can self-refer to a clinic. In other areas of Wales, you can ask your GP for a direct referral to an endo nurse.
In England and Scotland, access is typically through an accredited Endometriosis Centre. If you're a patient at one, you should have access to a specialist nurse.
To find a list of official centres, search the British Society for Gynaecological Endoscopy (BSGE) website for their directory.
The situation in Northern Ireland is tougher. There is only one accredited centre and, as of late 2023, only one specialist endo nurse.
Be aware: Specialist nurses are a vital resource, but they manage huge workloads. There may be a wait for an appointment.
A nurse is a 'good band-aid', but not the only care you need. Comprehensive endo management often involves a full team: dieticians, psychologists, pelvic physios, and consultants.

outline.key_point: Private endo care isn't just about 'skipping the queue'. It's about the kind of team you get.
The NHS provides specialised endometriosis centres. The pathway usually starts with your GP, but wait times for a specialist can be long.
The private route can offer faster access to advice and treatment, sometimes with the option to self-refer to a specialist.
But a key difference isn't just speed. It's how the care team is structured.
Leading private centres often have a multidisciplinary team: colorectal surgeons, urologists, pain specialists, and an endo nurse working together.
This integrated approach means complex symptoms involving the bowel or bladder are assessed by the right experts from the start.
The right choice depends on your symptoms, finances, and urgency. Both pathways can lead to excellent care, but they work differently.
Whichever route you consider, the first step is organising your symptoms for a clear conversation with your GP or a specialist.

outline.key_point: The postcode lottery for endo care is real. But you can find the accredited centres.
Access to specialist endometriosis care shouldn't depend on where you live. Yet for many, it does, leading to long waits and travel for treatment.
To standardise care, the British Society for Gynaecological Endoscopy (BSGE) accredits specialist endometriosis centres across the UK.
These accredited centres have multidisciplinary teams, including an endometriosis nurse specialist, and are equipped to handle complex surgery.
You can find the official list of all accredited centres in England, Wales, Scotland, and Northern Ireland on the BSGE website.
How you get seen varies. In some areas, like Cardiff, you may be able to self-refer to a clinic. In most, you will need a referral from your GP or gynaecologist.
The network is growing. Wales, for example, now has three BSGE centres, so fewer people need to travel all the way to Cardiff for their surgery.
Knowing where these centres are helps you have more informed conversations with your GP about your referral options.
Need help preparing for that conversation? DocMap can help you structure your symptoms and history. Tap the link in bio to get started.
